lens.included_activities: US full-service boat dealerships in the lower-middle-market band that sell new or pre-owned boats and also provide repeat customer services such as maintenance and repair, parts support, brokerage or consignment, storage, marina services, financing, insurance, or extended-service administration.
lens.excluded_activities: Pure inventory retailers with no material repeat service relationship, boat manufacturers, stand-alone marinas, stand-alone repair shops classified elsewhere, distributors, captive manufacturer outlets, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Predominantly consumer-facing boat and accessory sales, with recurring or repeat revenue from service labor, parts, storage, brokerage, finance and insurance commissions, warranties, and other ancillary services. Boat sales are transactional and cyclical; service and ownership-lifecycle revenue is the recurring component relevant to the lens.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code combines transactional merchandise sellers with full-service dealerships. The lens is narrowed to dealers with a material repeat service or ownership-lifecycle relationship because pure boat retail alone does not supply a recurring outsourced service.

a.bridge: Marine-dealer sources document automatable workflows and dealer pain points rather than a wage-weighted task census. I map AI-assisted listings, lead qualification, quoting, marketing content, document preparation, scheduling, and routine customer updates to sales, marketing, office, and service-administration labor, while treating technicians, delivery, inspection, and relationship selling as much less substitutable. This may overstate exposure where small-dealer roles are blended and understate it where administrative work is especially manual.
a.rationale: Purpose-built marine software already offers AI listings and sales agents, digital quotes, marketplace syndication, CRM workflows, and pre-filled closing packages. Dealer survey evidence also identifies time and staffing as the leading digital-marketing pain point and incomplete API adoption, indicating both meaningful clerical and commercial-task exposure and a still-manual baseline. The physical and trust-intensive portions of sales and service cap the exposed share.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit OEWS staffing table combined with a structured task diary from salespeople, service advisers, title clerks, marketers, technicians, and managers at 30-50 independent boat dealers would materially move this estimate.
caveats: No source measures wage-weighted AI task exposure specifically for NAICS 441222.
The MRAA survey concerns digital marketing rather than the whole dealership labor pool.
Vendor capability claims show technical availability, not realized labor substitution.

rho.rationale: More than half of the exposed opportunity should be implementable within five years because dealer-specific products already integrate inventory, CRM, quoting, documents, and marketplaces. Implementation remains constrained by fragmented data, OEM and state paperwork, variable digital maturity, seasonality, customer preference for human advice, and the need to retain staff for exception handling and peak periods.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after payroll hours, lead-response times, quote-cycle times, error rates, and adoption cohorts from a marine DMS vendor across at least 50 independent dealerships would materially narrow the range.
caveats: Existing product features do not establish sustained adoption or headcount avoidance.
Small dealerships may lack clean inventory and customer data or integration staff.
Physical technician work and accountable deal approval remain outside the implementation case.

e.rationale: A substantial but not universal share of LMM boat dealers should have a material repeat service or ownership-lifecycle business. OneWater describes repair, maintenance, storage, marina, financing, insurance, parts, and rentals alongside boat sales, while also noting that independent retailers predominantly focus on new boats and often have less non-boat capability. The midpoint therefore excludes many sales-led dealers without assuming the full-service model is rare.
e.change_evidence: A Census Business Register or commercial dealer census tagged by on-site service bays, storage slips, service revenue, and F&I activity for the 397 modeled LMM firms would directly measure eligibility.
caveats: OneWater is a large consolidator and is not representative of independent LMM dealers.
The provided firm count is margin-bridged and may include firms whose EBITDA arises mainly from inventory sales.
Ancillary activities can be present but too small to make the repeat-service lens economically meaningful.

s5.rationale: The industry is fragmented and mostly owner-operated, making succession and consolidation plausible, and an active dealership broker reports substantial recent transaction volume. However, that volume covers several dealership verticals and supplies no denominator for eligible marine firms. A moderate five-year transfer probability is therefore bounded judgment rather than an observed rate.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year closed-deal database from marine dealership brokers, matched to a denominator of eligible independent dealers and classified by control transfer versus minority recapitalization or internal reorganization, would directly estimate s5.
caveats: The reported 94 and 91 transactions cover automotive, RV, marine, powersports, trucking, and equipment dealerships across North America, not only US boat dealers.
Broker-reported activity may be selected toward marketable firms and does not reveal failed sale processes.
Owner age and succession readiness are not directly observed.

q.rationale: Efficiency gains in fixed-price administrative work, service scheduling, marketing, and internal paperwork can be retained for a time, particularly in higher-margin service and fee revenue. Retention is limited by intense dealer competition, transparent online inventory, negotiated boat pricing, OEM constraints, commission compensation, and renewal repricing. OneWater's 2025 results show boat-margin pressure while service, parts and other gross margin remained much higher, supporting a mixed capture profile.
q.change_evidence: Dealer-level gross-margin and pricing data for AI-adopting versus matched non-adopting locations over several renewal and selling seasons, split among boat sales, service labor, parts, storage, and F&I, would materially change q.
caveats: A public consolidator's segment economics may not match LMM independents.
Service, parts and other is a mixed category that includes distribution activity.
The estimate separates price retention from implementation and demand effects, but real dealer decisions can couple them.

d5.bridge: New-boat unit sales and public-company commentary proxy the broader constant-price, constant-quality demand basket of eligible full-service dealerships. I temper the 2024 sales decline with evidence that pre-owned activity remains above 2019 and historically stable through cycles, and that installed-base services are more resilient. This can understate service demand if owners keep boats longer and overstate it if affordability or participation structurally weakens.
d5.rationale: New powerboat retail sales fell 9% in 2024, showing acute cyclicality, while OneWater reports approximately 859,000 pre-owned boats sold in 2024 and pre-owned traditional powerboat sales above 2019 levels. The current service basket is supported by the installed fleet and repair, storage, parts, brokerage, and F&I needs, so flat real demand is a reasonable midpoint with a wide cyclical range.
d5.change_evidence: NMMA five-year forecasts and actual unit, participation, boat-age, service-work-order, storage-occupancy, and real service-spend series split by new boats, used boats, and maintenance would materially change the range.
caveats: New-boat unit sales are more cyclical than the full ownership-lifecycle service basket.
The sources do not hold quality and price constant exactly.
Interest rates, tariffs, weather, fuel costs, and consumer confidence can dominate a five-year period.

o.rationale: Most year-five quantity should still require an accountable dealership operator because boats require physical inventory custody, inspection, trade-in appraisal, delivery, titling, financing coordination, repair, parts, and warranty handling. Digital marketplaces, AI sales agents, and self-service quoting can eliminate portions of search, lead handling, and paperwork, but are unlikely to replace the operating entity for most transactions and physical services.
o.change_evidence: Consumer-path data from OEMs and marketplaces showing the share of transactions completed direct-to-consumer without a dealer, plus dealer work-order data separating remote/self-service from operator-performed steps, would materially move o.
caveats: OEM direct sales and mobile service could grow faster than assumed.
The operator requirement is stronger for repair and delivery than for search, marketing, and simple brokerage administration.
State franchise, title, and registration rules vary and may change.

narrative.executive_view: The relevant opportunity is not pure boat retail but the full-service dealership that owns the recurring customer relationship across maintenance, parts, storage, brokerage, financing, insurance, and future trade-ins. AI can reduce the administrative load around that relationship, while the physical asset and regulated transaction preserve a strong need for an operator. Demand and margins remain highly cyclical.
narrative.ai_impact: Dealer-specific software can already generate listings, qualify leads, answer inventory questions, produce quotes, syndicate inventory, prepare closing packets, and automate follow-up. Those tools target sales support, marketing, office, and service-adviser tasks; they do much less to substitute for diagnosis, repair, inspection, trade appraisal, delivery, and high-trust selling.
narrative.value_capture: Capture should be better in fixed-price service, fee, and administrative workflows than in heavily negotiated boat sales. Online price transparency and intense competition can pass savings to buyers, while higher-margin service and ownership-lifecycle revenue offers more room to retain productivity gains.
narrative.firm_availability: The market is fragmented and mostly owner-operated, and consolidation is demonstrably active. Eligibility is less certain than raw fragmentation because many independents remain predominantly new-boat sellers with limited ancillary capability; deal volume also lacks an industry-specific denominator.
narrative.demand_durability: Boat purchases are discretionary and rate-sensitive, but the installed fleet creates repeat maintenance, parts, storage, repair, and resale needs. Recent new-unit contraction warrants a broad range, while used-boat activity and ownership-lifecycle services reduce the risk that the whole current basket disappears.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The biggest risks are a prolonged affordability downturn, inventory and floorplan pressure, tariff exposure, severe weather, low digital maturity, poor data integration, and overestimating how many dealers have a material service relationship. The key evidence gaps are an occupation-weighted task map, eligible-dealer census, and denominator-based control-transfer history.
narrative.principal_driver: Existing marine-specific automation can remove substantial lead, listing, quote, marketing, and paperwork effort while physical boat service keeps the customer relationship operator-led.
narrative.principal_weakness: The code is dominated by cyclical merchandise sales, and neither full-service eligibility nor five-year transfer probability is directly measured for LMM firms.

sources.title: 2025 Marine Industry Digital Marketing Report
sources.supports: Dealer survey reports time/staffing as the most-cited digital-marketing pain point at 13.4%; 57% use an API/feed for external inventory, while 35% do not; it documents ongoing integration and automation gaps.

sources.title: Boatyard Sales: Marine Dealership Management Software
sources.supports: Documents currently offered marine-dealer workflows including AI-powered listings and sales agents, digital quoting, inventory and lead management, marketplace syndication, and automatic mapping of customer and boat data into closing documents.

sources.title: OneWater Marine Inc. 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Reports roughly 4,000 US dealerships, mostly owner-operated with three stores or fewer; describes full-service activities and independent retailers' limited non-boat capability; provides revenue, gross-margin, cyclicality, and used-boat evidence.

sources.title: The current state of the marine buy-sell market
sources.supports: A dealership broker reports 94 transactions in 2024 and 91 in 2025 across several North American dealership verticals and describes weaker recent activity, buyer caution, financing costs, and valuation pressure.

sources.title: NMMA Confirms 9% Decline in 2024 New Boat Retail Sales
sources.supports: NMMA reports a 9% decline in 2024 new powerboat retail unit sales and highlights uncertainty around participation and consumer behavior.
